title: Mountain range

infobox.value: or **hill range** is a series of [mountains](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain") or [hills](/wiki/Hill "Hill") arranged in a line and connected by high ground. A **mountain system** or **mountain belt** is a group of mountain ranges with similarity in form, structure, and alignment that have arisen from the same cause, usually an [orogeny](/wiki/Orogeny "Orogeny").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Mountain ranges are formed by a variety of geological processes, but most of the significant ones on Earth are the result of [plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Mountain ranges are also found on many planetary mass objects in the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") and are likely a feature of most [terrestrial planets](/wiki/Terrestrial_planet "Terrestrial planet").
["Definition of mountain system"](https://www.mindat.org/glossary/mountain_system). *Mindat.org*. Hudson Institute of Mineralogy. Retrieved 26 August 2017.

first_paragraph: Geographic area containing several geologically related mountains

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Geographic area containing several geologically related mountains

For financial options, see [Mountain range (options)](/wiki/Exotic_option "Exotic option").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Apollo_9_image_of_the_Namcha_Barwa_Himal_range%2C_AS09-23-3511.jpg/500px-Apollo_9_image_of_the_Namcha_Barwa_Himal_range%2C_AS09-23-3511.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apollo_9_image_of_the_Namcha_Barwa_Himal_range,_AS09-23-3511.jpg)

The [Namcha Barwa Himal](/wiki/Namcha_Barwa_Himal "Namcha Barwa Himal"), east part of the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") as seen from space by [Apollo 9](/wiki/Apollo_9 "Apollo 9")

A **mountain range** or **hill range** is a series of [mountains](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain") or [hills](/wiki/Hill "Hill") arranged in a line and connected by high ground. A **mountain system** or **mountain belt** is a group of mountain ranges with similarity in form, structure, and alignment that have arisen from the same cause, usually an [orogeny](/wiki/Orogeny "Orogeny").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Mountain ranges are formed by a variety of geological processes, but most of the significant ones on Earth are the result of [plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Mountain ranges are also found on many planetary mass objects in the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") and are likely a feature of most [terrestrial planets](/wiki/Terrestrial_planet "Terrestrial planet").

Mountain ranges are usually segmented by [highlands](/wiki/Highland "Highland") or [mountain passes](/wiki/Mountain_pass "Mountain pass") and [valleys](/wiki/Valley "Valley"). Individual mountains within the same mountain range do not necessarily have the same [geologic structure](/wiki/Structural_geology "Structural geology") or [petrology](/wiki/Petrology "Petrology"). They may be a mix of different orogenic expressions and [terranes](/wiki/Terrane "Terrane"), for example [thrust sheets](/wiki/Thrust_fault "Thrust fault"), [uplifted blocks](/wiki/Fault-block_mountain "Fault-block mountain"), [fold](/wiki/Fold_(geology) "Fold (geology)") mountains, and [volcanic](/wiki/Volcanic "Volcanic") landforms resulting in a variety of [rock types](/wiki/Lithology "Lithology").

## Major ranges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mountain_range&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Major ranges")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/World_Distribution_of_Mid-Oceanic_Ridges.gif/500px-World_Distribution_of_Mid-Oceanic_Ridges.gif)](/wiki/File:World_Distribution_of_Mid-Oceanic_Ridges.gif)

The [Ocean Ridge](/wiki/Mid-ocean_ridge#Global_system "Mid-ocean ridge"), the world's longest mountain range (chain)

Most geologically young mountain ranges on the Earth's land surface are associated with either the [Pacific Ring of Fire](/wiki/Pacific_Ring_of_Fire "Pacific Ring of Fire") or the [Alpide belt](/wiki/Alpide_belt "Alpide belt"). The Pacific Ring of Fire includes the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") of South America, extends through the [North American Cordillera](/wiki/North_American_Cordillera "North American Cordillera"), the [Aleutian Range](/wiki/Aleutian_Range "Aleutian Range"), on through [Kamchatka Peninsula](/wiki/Kamchatka "Kamchatka"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea"), to [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Andes is 7,000 kilometres (4,350 mi) long and is often considered the world's longest mountain system.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The Alpide belt stretches 15,000 km across southern [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia"), from [Java](/wiki/Java "Java") in Maritime [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") to the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") in [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"), including the ranges of the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas"), [Karakoram](/wiki/Karakoram "Karakoram"), [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush"), [Alborz](/wiki/Alborz "Alborz"), [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus_Mountains "Caucasus Mountains"), and the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The Himalayas contain the highest mountains in the world, including [Mount Everest](/wiki/Mount_Everest "Mount Everest"), which is 8,848 metres (29,029 ft) high.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Mountain ranges outside these two systems include the [Arctic Cordillera](/wiki/Arctic_Cordillera "Arctic Cordillera"), [Appalachians](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains"), [Great Dividing Range](/wiki/Great_Dividing_Range "Great Dividing Range"), [East Siberians](/wiki/East_Siberian_Mountains "East Siberian Mountains"), [Altais](/wiki/Altai_Mountains "Altai Mountains"), [Scandinavians](/wiki/Scandinavian_Mountains "Scandinavian Mountains"), [Qinling](/wiki/Qinling "Qinling"), [Western Ghats](/wiki/Western_Ghats "Western Ghats"), [Vindhyas](/wiki/Vindhya_Range "Vindhya Range"), [Byrrangas](/wiki/Byrranga_Mountains "Byrranga Mountains"), and the [Annamite Range](/wiki/Annamite_Range "Annamite Range"). If the definition of a mountain range is stretched to include underwater mountains, then the [Ocean Ridge](/wiki/Ocean_Ridge "Ocean Ridge") forms the longest continuous mountain system on Earth, with a length of 65,000 kilometres (40,400 mi).[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mountain_range&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Andes_70.30345W_42.99203S.jpg/250px-Andes_70.30345W_42.99203S.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andes_70.30345W_42.99203S.jpg)

The [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes"), the longest mountain range on the surface of the Earth, have a dramatic impact on the climate of South America

The position of mountain ranges influences climate, such as rain or snow.[[8]](#cite_note-8) When air masses move up and over mountains, the air cools, producing [orographic](/wiki/Orography "Orography") precipitation (rain or snow). As the air descends on the leeward side, it warms again (following the [adiabatic lapse rate](/wiki/Adiabatic_lapse_rate "Adiabatic lapse rate")) and is drier, having been stripped of much of its moisture. Often, a [rain shadow](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow") will affect the leeward side of a range.[[9]](#cite_note-9) As a consequence, large mountain ranges, such as the Andes, compartmentalize continents into distinct [climate regions](/wiki/Climate_regions "Climate regions").

## Erosion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mountain_range&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Erosion")]

Mountain ranges are constantly subjected to [erosional](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion") forces which work to tear them down.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The [basins](/wiki/Sedimentary_basin "Sedimentary basin") adjacent to an eroding mountain range are then filled with sediments that are buried and turned into [sedimentary rock](/wiki/Sedimentary_rock "Sedimentary rock"). Erosion is at work while the mountains are being uplifted until the mountains are reduced to low hills and plains.

The early [Cenozoic](/wiki/Cenozoic "Cenozoic") uplift of the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") of Colorado provides an example. As the uplift was occurring some 10,000 feet (3,000 m) of mostly [Mesozoic](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic") sedimentary [strata](/wiki/Stratum "Stratum") were removed by erosion over the core of the mountain range and spread as sand and clays across the [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains") to the east.[[11]](#cite_note-11) This mass of rock was removed as the range was actively undergoing uplift. The removal of such a mass from the core of the range most likely caused further uplift as the region adjusted [isostatically](/wiki/Isostasy "Isostasy") in response to the removed weight.

Rivers are traditionally believed to be the principal cause of mountain range erosion, by cutting into bedrock and transporting sediment. Computer simulation has shown that as mountain belts change from tectonically active to inactive, the rate of erosion drops because there are fewer abrasive particles in the water and fewer landslides.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Extraterrestrial "Montes"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mountain_range&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Extraterrestrial \"Montes\"")]

Further information: [List of tallest mountains in the Solar System](/wiki/List_of_tallest_mountains_in_the_Solar_System "List of tallest mountains in the Solar System")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Montes_Apenninus_AS15-M-1423.jpg/250px-Montes_Apenninus_AS15-M-1423.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montes_Apenninus_AS15-M-1423.jpg)

[Montes Apenninus](/wiki/Montes_Apenninus "Montes Apenninus") on the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") was formed by an impact event.

Mountains on other planets and [natural satellites](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite") of the Solar System, including the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"), are often isolated and formed mainly by processes such as impacts, though there are examples of mountain ranges (or "Montes") somewhat similar to those on Earth. [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn")'s moon [Titan](/wiki/Titan_(moon) "Titan (moon)")[[13]](#cite_note-MitriBland2010-13) and [Pluto](/wiki/Pluto_(dwarf_planet) "Pluto (dwarf planet)"),[[14]](#cite_note-NASA-20150724-lg-14) in particular, exhibit large mountain ranges in chains composed mainly of ices rather than rock. Examples include the [Mithrim Montes](/wiki/Mithrim_Montes "Mithrim Montes") and [Doom Mons](/wiki/Doom_Mons "Doom Mons") on Titan, and [Tenzing Montes](/wiki/Tenzing_Montes "Tenzing Montes") and [Hillary Montes](/wiki/Hillary_Montes "Hillary Montes") on Pluto. Some terrestrial planets other than Earth also exhibit rocky mountain ranges, such as [Maxwell Montes](/wiki/Maxwell_Montes "Maxwell Montes") on [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus") taller than any on Earth[[15]](#cite_note-KeepHansen1994-15) and [Tartarus Montes](/wiki/Tartarus_Montes "Tartarus Montes") on [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars").[[16]](#cite_note-Plescia2003-16) Jupiter's moon [Io](/wiki/Io_(moon) "Io (moon)") has mountain ranges formed from tectonic processes including the [Boösaule](/wiki/Bo%C3%B6saule_Montes "Boösaule Montes"), Dorian, Hi'iaka and [Euboea Montes](/wiki/Euboea_Montes "Euboea Montes").[[17]](#cite_note-Jaeger2003-17)
